paper_title: Reinforcement Learning from Rich Feedback with Distributional DAgger

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Reasoning models have advanced rapidly, but the dominant reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) recipe remains surprisingly narrow: sample many responses and reward each with a single bit indicating whether the final answer is correct. Yet many settings provide rich feedback, including execution traces, tool outputs, expert corrections, and model self-evaluations. We study how to use such feedback through a distributional variant of the classic imitation learning algorithm DAgger, where the learner has local access to an expert distribution on states visited by the current policy. This yields a simple forward cross-entropy objective that admits a blackbox expert and whose sequence-level gradient {conduct rich credit assignment by propagating} future expert-student disagreement back to earlier decisions. We show that prior RL with self-distillation objectives based on reverse KL or Jensen-Shannon fail to guarantee monotonic policy improvement: even when the expert has higher reward, their updates may increase probability on worse actions. In contrast, we show that forward cross-entropy admits monotonic policy improvement and enjoys guarantees on regret. We further show that our objective optimizes a lower bound on teacher-weighted likelihood of success, leading to improved Pass@N. Empirically, our approach, DistIL, improves over RLVR and RL with self-distillation baselines across a variety of domains: scientific reasoning, coding, and solving hard mathematical problems.

In contrast, we show that forward cross-entropy admits monotonic policy improvement and enjoys guarantees on regret. sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Reasoning large language models have made significant recent progress (Jaech et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2025; Comanici et al., 2025; Olmo et al., 2025). The dominant paradigm, reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves reasoning by sampling many candidate solutions from the model and providing each with single bit of feedback: whether the final answer is correct. This simple paradigm has been remarkably successful in domains such as coding and mathematical and scientific reasoning tasks where final answers can be checked automatically (Tan et al., 2025; Yue et al., 2025; Zeng et al., 2025; Pan and Liu, 2025; Luo et al., 2025). However, RLVR has important limitations. scalar terminal reward is typically broadcast across the entire response, causing every token to receive the same weight regardless of its actual contribution. This provides little credit assignment: it does not reveal which tokens, phrases, or reasoning steps caused the success or failure. Moreover, the paradigms reliance on automatic final-answer verification makes it difficult to apply in unverifiable domains, where final answer correctness cannot be easily checked. Many important settings, however, provide feedback that is far richer than single correctness bit: error logs, unit-test results, natural-language critiques, hints, or ground-truth responses. Inspired by the seminal work on knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015), growing line of research seeks to exploit these signals through on-policy self-distillation, in which the current model is conditioned on feedback to produce privileged teacher, whose distribution is then distilled back into the original policy (Hübotter et al., 2026; Zhao et al., 2026). This turns sparse outcome supervision into dense token-level guidance. In particular, viewed through the lens of policy gradients, these objectives induce an implicit form of credit assignment: different tokens receive different weights, e.g., determined by the local discrepancy between the student and feedback-conditioned teacher policies (Hübotter et al., 2026).

sections.title: 1.1 Contributions
sections.content: This motivates us to take closer look at on-policy self-distillation. Many classical policy-optimization methodssuch as conservative policy iteration (Kakade and Langford, 2002), natural policy gradient (Kakade, 2001), and trust-region policy optimization (Schulman et al., 2015)are designed around monotonic policy improvement: under an idealized update, such as sufficiently small natural-gradient or trust-region step, each update should increase policys average reward. This raises natural question for on-policy self-distillation. If feedback-conditioned teacher achieves higher average reward than the student, should an idealized update that pulls the student toward the teacher improve the student? We show that, for on-policy self-distillation objectives with -divergences, the answer is in general, no. To address this, we revisit the classical DAgger framework (Ross et al., 2011) and propose distributional variant that enjoys monotonic policy-improvement and regret guarantees, while empirically outperforming existing methods. Concretely, we make the following contributions: Provable limitations of existing on-policy self-distillation. We begin by analyzing on-policy self-distillation objectives based on general -divergences, class that subsumes existing objectives such as reverse-KL divergence (Hübotter et al., 2026) and JensenShannon divergence (Zhao et al., 2026). We identify two limitations. First, we prove that optimizing an -divergence does not, in general, guarantee monotonic policy improvement; even when the teacher is strictly better than the student and the update is an idealized natural-gradient step, the objective can increase probability on suboptimal actions (Propositions 1, 2). Second, existing methods typically rely on approximate gradients that provide only local credit assignment, measuring teacherstudent discrepancy at individual tokens. We show that this local approximation ignores how early token choices shape the future states where teacherstudent disagreement appears, erasing precisely the delayed feedback needed to credit earlier decisions. We prove that this can cause learning to converge to strictly suboptimal policies (Proposition 3). DistIL: new algorithm for RL with rich feedback. To address these limitations, we propose DistIL, distributional variant of the DAgger imitation-learning algorithm. DistIL optimizes forward cross-entropy loss between the feedback-conditioned teacher and the student policy, on states visited by the student (c.f. Equation (4)). Unlike reverse-KL or JensenShannon self-distillation objectives, DistIL can leverage teacher log-probabilities when available, but does not require them and its objective can be estimated using samples from the teacher alone, and therefore naturally accommodates black-box teachers such as external models or 2 human experts. Furthermore, DistIL performs future-aware credit assignment: teacherstudent disagreement at later timesteps can be propagated back to the earlier decisions (c.f. Equation (5)). DistIL enjoys theoretical guarantees. Beyond algorithmic simplicity, DistIL comes with several theoretical guarantees that explain why the objective is well aligned with reward improvement. First, we show that DistILs forward cross-entropy loss guarantees monotonic-improvement property missing from prior self-distillation objectives: under standard local assumptions, moving toward better teacher improves the students expected reward (Proposition 4). Second, we prove that DistIL achieves sublinear regret (Theorem 1). Finally, we show that DistIL maximizes teacher-weighted lower bound on the expected log-likelihood of success, drawing connections to maximum-likelihood RL (Tajwar et al., 2026) and providing principled explanation for improvements in pass@N for any (Proposition 5, c.f. Figure 1). Empirical results. The theoretical advantages of DistIL translate into consistent empirical gains. Across scientific reasoning (Feng et al., 2024), coding (Jain et al., 2024), and challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, DistIL outperforms strong RL and self-distillation baselines, including SDPO (Hübotter et al., 2026), OPSD (Zhao et al., 2026), and GRPO (Shao et al., 2024). The gains hold across different feedback regimes, from sparse final-answer correctness to execution traces and ground-truth solutions, highlighting the practical benefit of pairing reward-aligned objective with future-aware credit assignment.

sections.title: 2 Background and Problem Formulation
sections.content: Contextual Markov decision process and reinforcement learning. We model consider finite-horizon contextual Markov decision processes as the environment. At the beginning of each episode, context (i.e., prompt/problem) ρ is sampled. The initial state is set to be the context s1 = (x, ). At each timestep [H], the policy selects an action yt π( st), and the environment transitions to the next state. In the autoregressive language-model setting considered in this work, the transition is deterministic and the next state is obtained by appending the sampled action to the current prefix. Thus, we write st = (x, y1:t1). For simplicity, we sometimes write the entire trajectory as = (y1, . . . , yH ). Given per-step rewards rt(x, y1:t), the goal of reinforcement learning is to learn policy maximizing expected cumulative reward: J(π) := ExρEyπ(x) " # rt(x, y1:t) . (1) t=1 Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). In many reasoning tasks, rewards can be obtained from automatic verifiers: code can be checked with unit tests, while in some mathematical and scientific problems final answer correctness can be checked. This gives rise to reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where supervision is typically sparse and outcome-based: rt = 0 for < H, and the terminal reward rH evaluates the final answer, often as binary correctness signal. Methods such as GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) and its variants (Liu et al., 2025; Ahmadian et al., 2024) optimize policies using such rewards. Despite its simplicity and empirical success, RLVR provides only delayed supervision. Furthermore, the terminal reward is broadcast across the response, giving every token the same weight regardless of its role in the reasoning process. This makes credit assignment difficult. Moreover, in group-relative or advantage-based updates (Shao et al., 2024), the learning signal can vanish when all sampled responses receive identical outcomes, such as all correct or all incorrect. Learning from rich feedback via distillation. In many settings, supervision extends beyond final-answer correctness and includes richer signals such as execution traces, error messages, expert annotations, or feedback from other models. These signals provide more informative guidance and can be leveraged to improve learning. Inspired by knowledge distillation, recent line of work studies variants of on-policy distillation, where student policy πS is trained to match teacher policy πT along trajectories sampled from the student (Agarwal et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2025a; Xiao et al., 2026; Zeng et al., 2026). In on-policy self -distillation, the teacher is derived from the same model but conditioned on additional feedback : πT ( x, y1:t1) := πθ( x, f, y1:t1), πS( x, y1:t1) := πθ( x, y1:t1), where πT denotes the feedback-conditioned teacher and πS denotes the student policy. Given trajectories πS( x), training minimizes Lself-distill := Exρ, yπS (x) D(πS( x, y1:t1) πT ( x, y1:t1)) , (2) t=1 where is divergence measure, such as reverse KL in SDPO (Hübotter et al., 2026) or JensenShannon divergence in OPSD (Zhao et al., 2026). Problem formulation: RL from rich feedback. We now formalize the reinforcement-learning problem considered in this paper. We work with the deterministic contextual MDP introduced above, and let πθ Π denote policy parameterized by θ. As in the self-distillation setting, at each training round rich feedback induces privileged teacher policy πT , which is held fixed during the student update. Our central assumption is that this feedback is useful: for some underlying, possibly unknown reward function, the feedback-induced teacher is at least as good as the current unconditioned student in expected reward. Formally, for the student policy πθ, we assume := Exρ,,yπT (x) [r(x, y)] Exρ,,yπθ(x) [r(x, y)] 0. (3) For example, the above assumption says that model conditioned on execution logs, critiques, hints, or correct solution should, on average, achieve higher success rate than the same model without access to that feedback. This assumption is precisely what makes the self-distillation paradigm meaningful; if the privileged teacher were not better aligned with the task reward than the student, then distilling the teacher would have no reason to improve the student. Since the reward may be unknown, the learning algorithms do not optimize the reward objective directly and they instead optimize distillation-based surrogate losses. Notation. sections.title: 3 Limitations of Existing On-Policy Self-Distillation Methods
sections.content: The formulation above raises the following question: if rich feedback induces better teacher, does selfdistillation necessarily turn this advantage into policy improvement? In this section, we start by analyzing the two consequential choices made in existing methods: the divergence measure used to distill the teacher, and the gradient estimator used to optimize it. We show that both choices can fail in principle, even in favorable, idealistic scenarios. First, we ask whether divergence minimization guarantees monotonic policy improvement when the teacher is better than the student (Section 3.1). Second, we show that the local, tokenwise gradients used in prior works (Zhao et al., 2026; Hübotter et al., 2026) can ignore how early decisions affect future teacherstudent disagreement, leading to strictly suboptimal policies (Section 3.2).

sections.title: 3.1 Does divergence minimization guarantee monotonic policy improvement?
sections.content: A useful property for policy optimization algorithms is monotonic policy improvement: under an idealized update, the policy should move in direction that increases expected reward. This principle underlies classical methods such as natural policy gradient (NPG) (Kakade, 2001), and is approximately enforced by widely used trust-region and proximal methods such as TRPO (Schulman et al., 2015), PPO (Schulman et al., 2017), and GRPO (Shao et al., 2024). This motivates the analogous question for self-distillation: if the teacher has higher expected reward than the student, does an ideal update that pushes the student toward the teacher necessarily improve the student? We answer this question for general -divergence self-distillation objectives, class that includes reverse KL as used in SDPO (Hübotter et al., 2026) and JensenShannon divergence as used in OPSD (Zhao et al., 2026). The proposition below shows that, even in contextual bandits, minimizing such objectives does not in general guarantee monotonic policy improvement. 4 Proposition 1 (f -divergence self-distillation does not guarantee monotonic policy improvement). Let πT be fixed teacher and πθ student policy. For the softmax parameterization, the Natural Policy Gradient (NPG) step minimizing an -divergence Df (πT πθ), i.e., θ = θ η (θ)1θDf (πT πθ), satisfies J(πθ) = J(πθ) η Covyπθ (r(y), g(y)) + O(η2), (cid:17) (cid:16) πT (y) πθ(y) πθ(y) (cid:16) πT (y) (cid:17) where g(y) = policy improvement if and only if Covyπθ gap > 0 alone does not guarantee monotonic policy improvement. . Consequently, for sufficiently small η > 0, the NPG step provides (r(y), g(y)) < 0. In particular, positive teacherstudent reward πT (y) πθ(y) detailed proof can be found in Appendix A.1. Proposition 1 highlights simple but important distinction: teacher can be better on average, while the update induced by distillation objective can still point in reward-decreasing direction. For SDPO, which corresponds to reverse KL, the first-order change in reward is governed by Covyπθ (cid:18) r(y), log πθ(y) π(y) (cid:19) . This covariance measures how the studentteacher mismatch aligns with reward. Importantly, the covariance is not determined by the teachers average advantage , defined in (3). Instead, it depends on where the student and teacher differ. Reverse KL improves the student only when the trajectories over-weighted by the student relative to the teacher are, on average, low-reward. If the student over-weights trajectories that are actually high-reward, the same update may suppress good behavior and decrease expected reward. The failure can already occur in three-action bandit. Imagine three trajectories: excellent (r = 1), mediocre (r = 0.5), and bad (r = 0). The teacher may be better overall because it puts more probability on the excellent trajectory, but it also puts far less probability than the student on the mediocre trajectory. reverse-KL update then treats the students extra mass on the mediocre trajectory as an error to be corrected; after normalization, this can increase probability on the bad trajectory even though it has zero reward. Thus, being better on average is not enough and the distillation direction must also be aligned with reward. The next proposition makes this construction concrete and formally proves the failure result for reverse-KL distillation. Proposition 2 (Reverse-KL distillation can decrease reward). There exist universal constants c0, c1 > 0 and three-armed bandit with rewards in [0, 1], fixed teacher policy πT , and student policy πθ such that the teacher is better than the student by constant margin c0 > 0 but the natural policy gradient step minimizing the reverse KL objective, decreases the students expected reward. In particular, for all sufficiently small η > 0, we have J(πθ) J(πθ) c1η. The detailed construction is given in Appendix A.2. It uses three-action bandit where the teacher has constant reward advantage over the student. Nevertheless, due to the normalization in the reverse-KL NPG update, the update can increase probability on low-reward action whose studentteacher log-ratio is below average. Thus, expected reward can decrease even though the teacher is better overall. Takeaway: Self-distillation with -divergence does not guarantee monotonic policy improvement. For -divergence distillation, policy improvement depends on whether the induced update direction is aligned with reward, not only on whether the teacher has higher expected reward. Thus, even when the teacher is strictly superior to the students, updates induced by reverse KL in SDPO and JensenShannon divergence in OPSD can degrade the student.

sections.title: 3.2 Local credit assignment leads to suboptimal policies
sections.content: Next, we examine the gradient approximations used in existing self-distillation methods such as SDPO and OPSD. In the self-distillation objective (2), the student policy πθ appears in two places: inside the divergence, and in the trajectory distribution that determines which states are visited. Keeping the teacher πT fixed, the 5 full gradient of this objective decomposes into local term and future-credit term: θLself-distill = θD(πθ( st)πT ( st)) t=1 {z :=local } + θ log πθ(yt st) D(πθ( sτ )πT ( sτ )) t=1 τ {z :=future , } where st = (x, y1:t1). The first term is the tokenwise distillation gradient, which updates the policy using only the local mismatch between the student and teacher at the current state. For example, under reverse KL, this term weights θ log πθ(y st) by the local log-ratio log πT (yst) , yielding an implicit form of token-level πθ(yst) credit assignment. SDPO and OPSD effectively approximate the full gradient by retaining only this local term, θLself-distill local. This drops the second term, which is precisely credit-to-go term: it assigns credit to an action at step according to the teacherstudent mismatch that appears at future states reached after taking that action. Without this term, the update can miss delayed feedback entirely, assigning no credit to early choices whose consequences appear only downstream. The next proposition proves that this local approximation can lead to strictly suboptimal policies. Proposition 3 (Local credit assignment can be strictly suboptimal). There exist two-step contextual MDP with action space = {a, b, c}, parametric student policy class {πu,v}u,vR, teacher policy πT , and reward function such that, starting from initialization (u, v) = (0, 0), the local credit assignment gradient converges to policy πlocal with J(πlocal) = 1 3 , whereas the full sequence-level gradient converges to policy 5 . Consequently, J(πseq) > J(πlocal), and local credit assignment is strictly suboptimal. πseq with J(πseq) = 2 Proposition 3 highlights fundamental limitation of local tokenwise updates: they may fail to assign credit to earlier decisions, leading to strictly suboptimal solutions. Consequently, methods such as SDPO and OPSD, which rely on such local gradient estimators, can fail to learn from delayed feedback. The construction in Appendix A.3 considers two-step problem in which the teacherstudent mismatch is encountered only along one branch of the decision tree. Under the local objective, the contribution of this mismatch is differentiated while treating the prefix distribution as fixed, giving zero gradient with respect to the first-step decision. As result, optimization remains at the symmetric initialization and produces policy with expected reward 1/3. In contrast, the full sequence-level objective accounts for how the first-step decision affects the distribution of future states. This induces nonzero gradient on the first-step parameter, allowing optimization to change the first-step decision and shift probability mass toward higher-reward actions and attain expected reward 2/5. Thus, local credit assignment can converge to strictly suboptimal policy even in simple two-step problem. Takeaway: Tokenwise local gradient approximation can fail to credit early decisions. SDPO and OPSD apply stop-gradient on the sampling process, so gradients pass only through the per-token divergence and may ignore how current token choices affect future token distributions. This can yield zero gradient for consequential early decisions while the full sequence-level gradient is nonzero, causing convergence to strictly suboptimal policies. Taken together, these results highlight two distinct limitations of existing self-distillation approaches: the objective may induce updates that are not monotonically reward-improving, and local tokenwise gradient approximations may miss the delayed consequences of early decisions. This suggests that learning from rich feedback requires both more reward-aligned objective and gradient estimator that preserves sequence-level credit assignment. We develop such an approach next.

sections.title: 4 RL from Rich Feedback with Distributional Imitation Learning
sections.content: Distributional imitation learning formulation. We view self-distillation through the lens of on-policy imitation learning, in the spirit of DAgger (Ross et al., 2011): the learner trains on states induced by its own policy, while an expert provides guidance on those visited states. This perspective is especially natural for learning 6 from rich feedback, where the student generates trajectories, and feedback induces privileged teacher that can be queried on the states the student encounters. We develop distributional variant of DAgger. In contrast to the standard setup where the expert supplies single action at each visited state, we allow the supervision to take the form of local teacher distribution: when available, we use πT ( st); when only samples are available, we estimate the objective using one or more samples yt πT ( st). Concretely, we propose the following distributional imitation-learning (DistIL) objective: LDistIL(θ) := Exρ, yπθ(x) " t=1 (πT ( x, y1:t1), πθ( x, y1:t1)) (4) # where we use πT ( x, y1:t1) = sg (πθ( x, y1:t1, )). Importantly, this objective does not require access to the teacher probabilities. It admits unbiased sample-based estimates, while also allowing teacher probabilities to be used when available. This flexibility allows DistIL to accommodate both black-box teachers, such as external models or human experts, as well as feedback-conditioned privileged teachers. This gives DistIL practical advantage over SDPO, whose objective requires access to teacher probabilities. Full gradients enabling future-aware credit assignment. Motivated by the failure of local gradient approximations established in Section 3.2, we optimize LDistIL(θ) defined in (4) using full gradients rather than treating the student-induced state distribution as frozen. In Appendix B.1, we show that the gradients of DistIL objectives are given by: θLDistIL = Exρ,yπθ(x) " θH (cid:16) πT ( st), πθ( st) # (cid:17) } t=1 {z local credit assignment " θ log πθ(at st) + Exρ,yπθ(x) t=1 {z future-credit assignment (cid:16) (cid:17) πT ( sτ ), πθ( sτ ) τ >t (5) !# . } The DistIL gradient decomposes into two complementary terms: teacher-weighted local imitation term and future-aware credit-assignment term. The first term provides teacher-weighted local imitation. At each visited state st, its inner gradient is θH (πT ( st), πθ( st)) = πT (y st)θ log πθ(y st), so the student increases token logprobabilities in proportion to the teachers probabilities. This teacher-weighted form is central to DistIL: unlike SDPO-style local updates, which weight student log-probabilities by studentteacher mismatch log πT (yst) , DistIL imitates the teacher distribution directly. This direct imitation is what yields the πθ(yst) reward-aligned update direction behind DistILs monotonic-improvement guarantee (c.f. Proposition 4). The same design also allows the gradient to be estimated from teacher samples when only black-box access is available. The second term provides future-aware credit assignment. It carries teacherstudent disagreement at later prefixes, measured by cross-entropy, back through θ log πθ(yt st) to the earlier tokens that made those prefixes likely. DistIL algorithm. Algorithm 1 provides practical variant of DistIL, used in our experiments. The algorithm instantiates the distributional imitation objective in (4) with the future-aware credit-assignment term in (5). In practice, we normalize the cumulative future loss to avoid length bias in long generations (Gu et al., 2024; Rashidinejad and Tian, 2025). We optimize the objective with PPO-style trust-region update. Following prior self-distillation methods, the teacher is obtained through an exponential moving average copy of the student (Hübotter et al., 2026) or fixed as the initial student parameters (Zhao et al., 2026), conditioned on the rich feedback. 7 Algorithm 1 Distributional Imitation Learning (DistIL) with Rich Feedback Require: Student policy πθ1 , teacher policy πϕ with parameters initialized at ϕ := θ1, prompt distribution ρ, learning rate η, number of iterations , group size G, update parameter β [0, 1]. sections.title: 5 Theoretical Properties of DistIL
sections.content: We now turn to the theoretical properties of DistIL. Our analysis shows that the same design choices that make DistIL natural for rich feedback also align it with reward improvement. The forward cross-entropy objective yields monotonic policy-improvement guarantee; the resulting online imitation-learning procedure admits sublinear regret bound; and the objective optimizes teacher-weighted lower bound on the maximum likelihood of success, explaining why DistIL can improve pass@n in practice.

sections.title: 5.1 DistIL enjoys monotonic policy improvement
sections.content: Section 3.1 showed that distilling better teacher via -divergences is not enough to guarantee policy improvement and the surrogate objective must also point in reward-improving direction. We now show that DistIL has precisely this property. Under fixed state distribution and mild local realizability condition, the natural-gradient step induced by the forward cross-entropy objective improves reward whenever the teacher is on average better than the student. The proof is deferred to Appendix B.3. Proposition 4 (DistIL enjoys monotonic policy improvement). Assume the map θ 7 πθ is twice continuously differentiable. Let denote the teacherstudent gap as defined in (3) and assume teacherstudent tangent space realizability: there exists Rd such that for all states and actions y, log πT (a s) log πθ(y s) = uθ log πθ(y s). Then, under fixed state distribution, the natural policy gradient step applied to DistIL θ = θ η (θ)1θH (πT πθ) with sufficiently small η > 0 satisfies: J(πθ) = J(πθ) + η + O(η2). Consequently, if > 0, the update gives monotonic policy improvement. Proposition 4 explains why the forward cross-entropy objective is aligned with reward improvement. Under fixed state distribution, DistILs natural-gradient update moves the student policy toward the teacher policy itself. In first order, πθ( s) becomes an interpolation between πθ( s) and πT ( s). Thus, when the teacher has higher expected reward, small movement toward the teacher improves the updated policy. Reverse-KL objective in SDPO does not have this property. Its update is weighted by the log-ratio log(πθ/πT ), so student does not necessarily move toward the teacher. As Section 3.1 shows, this weighting can suppress actions that are good but overrepresented by the student relative to the teacher, and can even increase probability on worse actions after normalization. This distinction is not related to the usual mode-seeking 8 versus mode-covering behavior of forward and reverse KL. The argument here is on first-order reward alignment: forward cross-entropy preserves the direction toward better teacher, while reverse KL can distort it. Remark. The tangent-space realizability assumption in Proposition 4 requires the teacher direction to be locally representable by the student policy class. In other words, the mismatch from the student toward the teacher must lie in the span of the students score functions. This is mild condition for expressive policy classes and holds exactly for tabular softmax policies; see Appendix B.2 for more details. Takeaway: DistILs update preserves the teacher direction Under the natural-gradient update, DistILs forward cross-entropy objective moves the student toward the teacher policy itself. This yields first-order policy improvement whenever the teacher is better on average. Reverse-KL objectives can distort this direction through log-ratio weighting, and therefore may not improve reward even when the teacher is better.

sections.title: 5.2 DistIL regret guarantee
sections.content: We next analyze DistIL as an online learning algorithm. Unlike the monotonic-improvement result, which studies single local update, the regret analysis accounts for the fact that each student update changes the state distribution on which the teacher is queried. The theorem below shows that the policy returned by DistIL achieves decaying suboptimality gap in terms of the expected reward relative to the teacher policy. The proof can be found in Appendix B.4.3. Theorem 1 (DistIL regret guarantee). Assume finite ratio-based concentrability coefficient < (Definition 1) and finite KL concentrability coefficient C0 < (Definition 2). Let σ2 denote the worst-case πT teacher-policy variance (Definition 3) and let µT denote the signed teacher recoverability parameter (Defq 2C0 inition 4). Then, the policy ˆπ returned by the NPG-DistIL Algorithm 2 with learning rate η = 1 satisfies: J(πT ) J(ˆπ) σ2 πT HC (cid:18) 2C0 (cid:19)1/4 + µT HC 2 2C0 . Theorem 1 makes explicit what controls the difficulty of learning from privileged teacher. The concentrability coefficients and C0 reflect the studentteacher coverage. The regret bound also contains two quantities that reflect the problem-dependent difficulty of conducting imitation learning in the environment. The variance measures the stochasticity of the teacher, i.e., how much the value of the teachers sampled actions term σ2 πT can fluctuate. The recoverability parameter µT measures the worst-case value loss from taking one arbitrary action at state and then following the teacher thereafter. When the teacher is nearly deterministic, the second term dominates and yields faster O(n1/2) rate. In contrast, for highly stochastic teachers, the variance term leads to slower O(n1/4) rate. Remark (Proof technique). The proof builds on the per-state mirror descent analysis of Chang et al. (2024), but requires non-trivial extension to the online setting. In particular, their analysis relies on expectations with respect to fixed distribution, which does not hold in our setting because the state distribution evolves after every student update. We handle this by deriving recurrence relation for the intermediate error terms and showing that the KL divergence contracts as the student is updated, allowing us to extend the analysis to the online regime. See Appendix B.4 for further details.

sections.title: 5.3 DistIL maximizes a teacher-weighted likelihood of success
sections.content: In this section, we show that DistIL can also be understood from sequence-level success perspective. While the previous results explain why the forward cross-entropy objective induces reward-aligned updates and admits monotonic policy improvement and regret guarantees, they do not yet explain why the method is particularly effective in sampling-based test-time metrics such as Pass@N and Majority@N (c.f., Figure 1). In the proposition below, we show that when rewards are binary indicators of success, optimizing DistILs distributional imitation objective also maximizes lower bound on teacher-weighted log-likelihood of success. 9 Proposition 5 (DistIL objective is lower bound on the teacher-weighted likelihood of success). For fixed prompt and any policy π, denote the success probability pπ(x) := Eyπ(x)[r(x, y)], where r(x, y) {0, 1} indicates correctness. Then (πT ( x), πθ( x)) pπT (x) log pπθ (x). In Proposition 5, the right-hand side is the students log probability of success on prompt x, weighted by the teachers probability of producing successful response on the same prompt. Thus, minimizing forward cross-entropy maximizes lower bound on teacher-weighted log-likelihood of success, placing more weight on prompts where the feedback-conditioned teacher is likely to be correct. The proof, given in Appendix B.5.1, follows by decomposing the teacher cross-entropy into correct and incorrect responses and lower bounding the contribution of correct responses, while noting that the remaining terms are nonnegative. This connection is specific to the teacher-weighted forward cross-entropy structure of DistIL. In contrast, objectives such as SDPO need not yield the same lower bound on the students probability of success. See Appendix B.5.2 for formal statement and analysis. Recent work on maximum-likelihood RL shows that maximizing the likelihood of successful trajectories improves Pass@N for all (Tajwar et al., 2026). Proposition 5 therefore provides principled explanation for DistILs strong Pass@N performance.

sections.title: 6 Experimental results
sections.content: We evaluate our method across three representative settings that capture different feedback regimes and levels of supervision: (i) science reasoning benchmarks without rich environment feedback; (ii) coding tasks with rich execution-based feedback; and (iii) mathematical reasoning on challenging problems with access to ground-truth solutions.

sections.title: 6.1 Bootstrapping on model generated correct responses as feedback
sections.content: We evaluate in the standard RLVR setting, where only binary success signal is observed per rollout. Following SDPO, we sample multiple rollouts per input and use successful trajectories as feedback , training the model to increase their likelihood and converting sparse rewards into dense, self-supervised signal. Setup. We compare against SDPO, on-policy GRPO (trajectories from the current policy), and off-policy GRPO (past rollouts reweighted via importance sampling). For all baselines, we adopt the same hyperparameter settings as in SDPO. Scientific reasoning is assessed on undergraduate-level problems in chemistry, physics, biology, and materials science using the L3 reasoning subset of SciKnowEval (Feng et al., 2024), with traintest split for in-domain generalization. We initialize from Qwen38B (Yang et al., 2025a) and Olmo37B-Instruct (Olmo et al., 2025), and report Best@16 and Maj@16 (Figure 1) and Avg@16  (Table 1)  against wall-clock training time using the verl framework (Sheng et al., 2025). Empirical Findings. Table 1 and Figure 1 summarize our results. DistIL achieves the best avg@16 performance in the large majority of columns across both model families, with the largest gains in physics and chemistry domains; physics gains reach 8.1/9.6 points at 5h over next-best on Qwen3-8B/OLMo3. Beyond accuracy, DistILs lead emerges within 20 steps and is largely monotonically sustained (Proposition 4), while SDPO exhibits instability: Best@16 collapses on biology after step 100 and oscillates on chemistry and physics. DistILs Best@16 advantage is grounded in Proposition 5: our forward cross-entropy objective maximizes teacher-weighted lower bound on success probability, implicitly optimizing Pass@N for all (Tajwar et al., 2026). On-policy GRPO saturates within one hour; off-policy GRPO lags DistIL by 5+ points on reasoning-heavy domains at 5h.

sections.title: 6.2 Learning with rich environment feedback
sections.content: We evaluate in environments with rich feedback, where signals go beyond binary success, such as execution errors, which standard RLVR cannot exploit. Following SDPO, we sample multiple rollouts per input and use execution-based feedback to construct supervision. Concretely, rollouts are evaluated against public unit tests, and feedback is derived from the resulting code execution logs. 10 Table 1 Comparison on scientific reasoning benchmarks (SciKnowEval L3). We report best avg@16 within 1h and 5h of wall-clock training on 4 NVIDIA H200 GPUs. Average is computed over all four domains. Bold denotes the best result per column; underline denotes the second best. We compare against SDPO and GRPO with identical hyperparameters as in SDPO on LiveCodeBench (LCBv6) (Jain et al., 2024), using public tests for training feedback and private tests for evaluation, initialized from Qwen3-8B (Yang et al., 2025a). Following (Jain et al., 2024), we report exact correctness by determining whether all test cases are passed (Score/Avg@k, Score/Maj@k, Score/Best@k) and partial correctness: fraction of test cases passed (Accuracy/Avg@k, Accuracy/Maj@k, Accuracy/Best@k), at temperature 0.2, top_p 0.95. Empirical Findings. DistIL achieves Accuracy/Avg@16 = 0.656 and Score/Avg@16 = 0.482, outperforming SDPO (0.643, 0.467) and GRPO (0.600, 0.405). The large gap over GRPO reflects its fundamental inability to exploit execution feedback. Figure 2 further shows consistent gains across all on both Best@k and Maj@k for score and accuracy, with the advantage most pronounced at small k. The gap between DistIL and SDPO could be attributed to our theoretically grounded-objective and squence-level credit assignment. Figure 2 LCBv6 evaluation at τ =0.2 for checkpoint at step-80 (following SDPO), reporting Score and Accuracy at Best@k and Maj@k for {2, 4, 8, 16}.

sections.title: 6.3 Learning to solve hard mathematical reasoning problems
sections.content: We evaluate with access to ground-truth solutions as feedback on very hard mathematical reasoning problems, enabling learning on problems where the base model never produces correct rollout. In this regime, RLVR methods such as GRPO receive zero advantage and cannot improve, while distillation-based approaches directly leverage ground-truth solutions to make progress. Setup. We construct challenging training set of 738 hard mathematical problems sourced from (CMU AIRe Lab, 2026) and OmniMath (problems with pass@512 = 0 for Qwen3-4B-Instruct), following (Setlur et al., 2026; Qu et al., 2026). We compare against OPSD (forward-KL), SDPO (reverse-KL), GRPO, and SFT, initialized from Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 models. Evaluation covers AIME24, AIME25, HMMT25, AMC23, and Minerva. Empirical Findings. Table 2 summarizes results. GRPO exactly matches the base model on both scales, confirming the expected failure: zero pass rate yields zero advantage. SFT degrades below the base model on most benchmarks, indicating overfitting rather than generalizable reasoning. DistIL achieves the best results 11 across the large majority of columns at both model scales. Gains are most substantial on AIME25, where DistIL leads the next-best method on Avg@16 by 3.8 points on Qwen3-4B and 1.4 points on Qwen3-8B. On the stronger Qwen3-8B, where SDPO and OPSD are competitive, DistIL consistently ranks first or second, with no degradation relative to the base model. Table 2 Performance comparison on mathematical reasoning benchmarks for Qwen3 models. Sampling configuration is given in Appendix C.2. On scientific reasoning, DistIL achieves the best Avg@16 on both model families, with gains up to 9.6 points. On coding, DistIL leads on all Best@k and Maj@k metrics; GRPOs large gap reflects its inability to exploit execution feedback. On hard mathematical reasoning problems, where GRPO receives zero gradients and fails to improve the base model, DistIL surpasses on-policy distillation baselines in most settings, with gains up to 3.8 points on AIME25 Avg@16. These results verify the advantages of reward alignment (Proposition 4) and future-aware credit assignment of DistIL for learning from rich feedback.

sections.title: 6.4 Ablation Study
sections.content: 6.4.1 Credit Assignment We compare DistIL against CE (cross-entropy) baseline that retains only the cross-entropy term of the gradient, performing local credit assignment analogous to SDPO and OPSD. Figure 3 shows results on the Material domain. DistIL consistently outperforms CE throughout training, demonstrating that the full credit assignment across the entire response distribution rather than assigning it locally, is the key driver of performance gains. The CE baseline exhibits higher variance and fails to improve to the level of DistILs performance, suggesting that local credit assignment is insufficient for learning robust scientific reasoning. Takeaway: Future credit assignment is advantageous in DistIL. Replacing full sequence-level credit assignment with local token-wise gradients (CE baseline) leads to higher variance and strictly worse performance, confirming Proposition 3. 6.4.2 Top-1 v/s Top v/s Full distribution We ablate the number of teacher tokens used in the distillation objective across {1, 100, Full} on the Physics domain (Figure 4). Top-1 distillation, which aligns the student only to the single highest-probability teacher token, underperforms throughout training, reflecting insufficient signal from single token. Top-100 achieves the best final performance and most stable training trajectory, outperforming both Top-1 and Full distillation. Full distillation, while competitive, introduces noise from low-probability tokens that are unlikely 12 Figure 3 Comparison between DistIL (Ours) and CE. The difference between these methods lies in the credit assignment. CE only does local token-wise creadit assignment similar to SDPO and OPSD, whereas DistIL does full credit assignment as given in Eq. (5). Figure 4 omparison of Avg@16 between different number of top-k tokens used for training DistIL on Material benchmark. to be informative, slightly degrading performance relative to Top-100. Based on this analysis, we use K=100 in all experiments.

sections.title: 7 Related Work
sections.content: Credit Assignment in RLVR. RLVR enables training from automatically verifiable outcomes without human preference annotations (Shao et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2025). However, supervision is applied at the trajectory level, assigning identical signals to all tokens despite their differing roles, making credit assignment especially challenging for long-horizon reasoning. One line of work addresses this by introducing intermediate supervision via process-reward models or step-wise evaluators (Lightman et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2026b; Luo et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2025b; Dai et al., 2025; Cui et al., 2025). While effective, these approaches require additional annotation or auxiliary models. An alternative direction avoids explicit supervision by reweighting updates using internal signals such as entropy or uncertainty, but these rely on heuristic notions of token importance (Xie et al., 2025; Li et al., 2026c; Cheng et al., 2026; Wang et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2025a; Sun et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2025b). In our work, instead of estimating token importance heuristically or learning separate reward models, we construct token-level supervision directly through privileged feedback. This yields structured credit assignment while preserving the simplicity of the verifier-only training pipeline. On-Policy Distillation and On-Policy Self-Distillation. On-policy distillation trains student on its own rollouts while teacher provides token-level supervision via divergence-based objectives (Agarwal et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2024; Lu and Lab, 2025; Xiao et al., 2026; Yang et al., 2025a). Aligning training with the students visitation distribution reduces the distribution mismatch inherent in off-policy approaches, but it typically relies on stronger external teacher. Recent work extends this paradigm to self-distillation, where single model provides both supervision and updates. OPSD (Zhao et al., 2026) formulates this as minimizing the divergence between privileged teacher, which is conditioned on ground-truth reasoning traces, and student, thereby improving token efficiency. SDPO (Hübotter et al., 2026) generalizes this setup to incorporate arbitrary feedback signals (e.g., code execution error logs), while RLSD (Yang et al., 2026a) integrates self-distillation with the GRPO methodologically to stabilize training. However, self-distillation introduces new challenges. It can degrade reasoning by suppressing epistemic uncertainty (Kim et al., 2026), and its success depends critically on alignment between teacher and student distributions as well as the quality of self-generated targets (Li et al., 2026b). Recent methods address these issues through improved supervision and routing strategies, including competence-aware weighting (PACED), iterative self-revision (SD-Zero), and hybrid routing between distillation and RL updates (SRPO) (Xu et al., 2026; He et al., 2026; Li et al., 2026a). In contrast, our approach revisits the objective underlying self-distillation. We adopt cross-entropy-based formulation with well-behaved optimization properties, resulting in more stable training dynamics and improved gradient estimation. This is related to methods such as DAgger (Ross et al., 2011) in imitation learning that query the expert for supervision on the learners induced state distribution.

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.content: This work studies reinforcement learning from rich feedback. We connect learning from rich feedback via distilling privileged feedback-conditioned teacher, to the standard expected-reward maximization objective in RL. This perspective raises natural question: if the feedback-conditioned privileged teacher is better than the student on average, can we design self-distillation algorithm whose updates monotonically improve the student toward the better teacher and admit regret guarantees? We show that existing self-distillation approaches can fail in two distinct ways: their objectives do not guarantee monotonic reward improvement and their local tokenwise gradient approximation can miss the delayed consequences of early decisions. In response, we introduce DistIL, distributional variant of DAgger that optimizes forward cross-entropy objective and performs future-aware credit assignment. We prove that DistIL enjoys monotonic policy improvement, admits regret guarantee, and implicitly optimizes teacher-weighted lower bound on the probability of success, explaining improvements in Pass@N for every . Empirically, we show that DistIL achieves strong performance across scientific reasoning, coding, and solving hard mathematical reasoning problems.

B.4 DistIL regret analysis This section proves the regret guarantee for DistIL. We begin by stating natural-policy-gradient variant of DistIL in Algorithm 2. We then provide definitions for quantities that appear in the analysis: ratio-based and KL-based concentrability (coverage) coefficients between the teacher and student policies (Rashidinejad et al., 2021; Chang et al., 2024) as well as two imitation-learning quantities that measure the difficulty of matching the teacher, namely teacher-policy variance and teacher recoverability parameter (Foster et al., 2024). With these definitions in place, Appendix B.4.3 proves the regret bound for DistIL. B.4.1 Natural-policy gradient variant of DistIL NPG-DistIL instantiates DistIL with natural policy gradient update rule. Rather than applying single global gradient step, the algorithm performs per-state mirror descent update following (Chang et al., 2024), where the mirror map is chosen to be the negative entropy, yielding the KL divergence as the associated Bregman divergence. Concretely, at each round i, the algorithm rolls out trajectories under the current student policy πθi , collects the set of visited state-action prefixes, and for each visited state minimizes linearization of the statewise cross-entropy loss ℓs subject to KL proximity penalty to the previous iterate πθi ( s). The full procedure is detailed in Algorithm 2. The teacher is typically feedback-conditioned variant of the student policy, often obtained from the initial student or from an exponential-moving-average copy of the student (Hübotter et al., 2026). Thus, the teacher and student are expected to have substantial support overlap at the start of training. Moreover, natural policy gradient and trust-region methods such as PPO constrain successive policy changes and help preserve this overlap across iterates. The constant µT measures recoverability: the largest value loss caused by taking an arbitrary action at state and then returning to the teacher thereafter. B.5.2 Reverse KL need not lower bound teacher-weighted likelihood of success In Proposition 5, we derived that DistIL objective is lower bound on the teacher-weighted likelihood of success and therefore achieves better Pass@N. Next, we show that this need not hold true for SDPO. Proposition 7 formalizes this distinction by providing counterexample in which the reverse-KL objective fails to provide such lower bound. The construction considers two-action setting in which the teacher assigns twice as much probability mass (2δ) to the correct response as the student (δ). As the teacher and student success probabilities simultaneously approach zero, the reverse-KL divergence scales as O(δ), whereas the magnitude of the teacher-weighted log-likelihood term, pπT (x), scales as Θ(δ log(1/δ)). Since δ log(1/δ) dominates δ as δ 0, the reverse-KL term becomes asymptotically negligible relative to the teacher-weighted log-likelihood term. Consequently, the reverse-KL objective need not provide lower bound on the teacher-weighted log-likelihood of success. sections.title: C Experiment Details
sections.content: We use 4 H200 GPUs for all our experiments. For prompts and implementation, we use SDPOs codebase (LAS Group, ETH Zurich, 2026) on Science and Coding benchmarks whereas OPSDs codebase (Zhao, 2026) on Mathematics benchmarks. For DistIL, we observe performance degradation for longer sequences πθ( x), as the cumulative future loss H() grows with sequence length, leading to disproportionately large gradient magnitudes. To mitigate this effect, we apply length normalization, similar to Gu et al. (2024). Specifically, we replace the cumulative loss in future-credit assignment in Eq. (5) with i>t Lt+1 = 1 1 i>t (stopgrad(πθ( x, y1:i1, )) πθ( si)). C.1 Hyperparameters for Science and Coding Benchmark For Science and Coding benchmarks, we follow the implementation of SDPO and use their reported hyperparameters for baselines. The hyperparameters for SDPO, DistIL (Ours), and GRPO with its off-policy and on-policy variants are provided in Table 3, 4, and 5 respectively. 33 Figure 5 LCBv6 evaluation at τ =0.2 (checkpoint-step 80), reporting Score and Accuracy at Best@k and Maj@k for {2, 4, 8, 16}. C.2 Hyperparameters for Maths task For Mathematics benchmarks, we follow the implementation of OPSD and use their reported hyperparameters for baselines. The hyperparameters for SDPO, OPSD, DistIL (Ours), and GRPO are provided in Table 6 and that for SFT in Table 7. Evaluation hyperparameters are provided in Table 8 for Qwen3-4B-2507-Instruct model and Table 9 for Qwen3-8B model. The only difference between OPSD and SDPO in this scenario is that OPSD uses Forward-KL divergence whereas SDPO uses reverse-KL divergence in these mathematics experiments.

sections.title: D Additional Results
sections.content: D.1 More results for Coding. SDPO evaluated their coding experiment at temperature = 0.6. We include results for that scenario in here. DistIL achieves Acc/Mean@16 = 0.660 and Score/Mean@16 = 0.482, outperforming SDPO (0.643, 0.467) and GRPO (0.639, 0.411). The large gap over GRPO reflects its fundamental inability to exploit execution feedback.
